name: lemon balm
mechanism: Proposed mechanism: nonclinical data include (a) inhibition of GABA transaminase in vitro with increased GABA levels, (b) CNS receptor binding activity in human brain-membrane assays (in vitro), and (c) sedative/anxiolytic-like effects in animal models; additionally, water extract may inhibit thyroid stimulating hormone activity in vitro/animals, with unknown clinical relevance.
activeCompounds: Essential oil constituents (citral/neral/citronellal), rosmarinic acid (phenylpropanoid), flavonoids (luteolin/quercetin/apigenin/kaempferol glycosides), triterpenes (ursolic/oleanolic acids) as listed in EMA assessment report.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless supervised by a qualified clinician.
Do not use with known allergy to this plant or related species.
safetyNotes: EMA monograph: pregnancy/lactation not recommended (insufficient data); may impair ability to drive/use machines.

sources.title: Community herbal monograph on *Melissa officinalis* L., folium (EMA)
EMA assessment report on *Melissa officinalis* L., folium

description: EMA assessment report lists key constituent categories: essential oil (with monoterpene aldehydes such as citral/neral/citronellal), flavonoids, phenylpropanoids (notably rosmarinic acid, with Ph. Eur. minimum content), tannins, and triterpenes.
dosage: EMA monograph (≥12 years): tea 1.5–4.5 g in 150 mL boiling water, 1–3×/day; powder 0.19–0.55 g 2–3×/day; liquid extract 2–4 mL 1–3×/day; tincture 2–6 mL 1–3×/day.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. ; nan; nan. No direct effects data. interactions: EMA monograph: "no data available" on interactions (not the same as "none").
preparation: Comminuted herbal substance (tea), powdered herb, liquid extract (DER 1:1; ethanol 45–53% v/v), tincture (1:5; ethanol 45–53% v/v), dried extracts corresponding to these preparations.
region: Native range (POWO): Mediterranean to Central Asia.
therapeuticUses: Traditional supportive use; indication depends on species part and preparation.
Evidence level varies; use only within established clinical or traditional context.
sideEffects: May cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or diarrhea).
Possible headache, dizziness, or allergic skin reaction in sensitive users.
summary: Traditional herbal medicinal product (EU) for relief of mild mental stress and to aid sleep; also for symptomatic treatment of mild GI complaints (bloating/flatulence), based on long-standing use.
